topic: Müller, K., & Zhang, L. (2023). Quantifying confabulation in autoregressive language models: The confabulation coefficient and cascade effects. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 78(4), 1123–1145. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2305.12345
final_review:
The concept of confabulation in autoregressive language models has gained significant attention in recent years, with researchers exploring its implications and potential applications (Müller & Zhang, 2023). One of the key aspects of confabulation is its relationship with memory retrieval, which has been studied in the context of ventromedial prefrontal cortex lesions (Asaf Gilboa et al., 2009). This research has shown that such lesions can produce early functional alterations during remote memory retrieval (Asaf Gilboa et al., 2009). In a similar vein, the role of belief in occurrence within autobiographical memory has been examined, highlighting the complex interplay between memory and belief (Scoboria et al., 2013).

The emergence of hallucination in large language models has also been a topic of interest, with some researchers defining and quantifying this phenomenon (Vipula Rawte et al., 2023). Others have explored the potential value of large language model hallucinations, arguing that they can have surprising benefits (Peiqi Sui et al., 2024). The use of large language models and large multimodal models in medical imaging has been discussed as a potential tool for physicians (Tyler Bradshaw et al., 2025). The application of conversational artificial intelligence in diagnostic settings has also been explored, with some researchers arguing that it has the potential to revolutionize the field (Tao Tu et al., 2025). However, the development of such systems requires careful consideration of their limitations and potential biases (Edisa Lozić et al., 2023).

The use of generative AI in creative problem-solving has also been examined, with some researchers arguing that it has the potential to enhance human creativity (Léonard Boussioux et al., 2024). Despite the potential benefits of large language models, there are also concerns about their accuracy and reliability, particularly in healthcare settings (Thomas Yu Chow Tam et al., 2024). The development of frameworks for human evaluation of large language models in healthcare has been proposed as a potential solution to this problem (Thomas Yu Chow Tam et al., 2024). The study of fractals in the nervous system has also been explored, with implications for theoretical neuroscience (Gerhard Werner, 2010). Furthermore, the concept of predictive brains and situated agents has been discussed in the context of cognitive science (Andy Clark, 2013).

In conclusion, the study of confabulation in autoregressive language models is a complex and multifaceted field, with implications for a range of areas, including memory retrieval, hallucination, and conversational artificial intelligence. Further research is needed to fully understand the potential benefits and limitations of large language models, particularly in healthcare settings (Asaf Gilboa et al., 2009; Scoboria et al., 2013; Vipula Rawte et al., 2023; Peiqi Sui et al., 2024; Tyler Bradshaw et al., 2025; Edisa Lozić et al., 2023; Léonard Boussioux et al., 2024; Thomas Yu Chow Tam et al., 2024; Gerhard Werner, 2010; Andy Clark, 2013; Müller & Zhang, 2023).
